sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 71-year-old male, presented with a 3-month history of a cough that started approximately 3 months ago while loading horses and improved with exercise, specifically biking.

He initially attributed the cough to a cold but was diagnosed with bronchitis by his primary care physician, who then referred him for a chest X-ray and subsequent CT scan that revealed a 2-cm solitary nodule in the right upper lobe.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
He has a history of smoking, having quit 7 years ago.
He has a family history of breast cancer in his mother but no history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis, bronchitis, and a history of smoking.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications: The patient takes methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:

Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/76 mmHg, heart rate is 70 beats per minute, respiration rate is 16 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation on room air is 98%, and temperature is within normal limits.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilateral, and the heart has a regular rate and rhythm with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities are pink, warm, and dry with no edema.
There is mild swelling in the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Pulses are intact in all extremities, and capillary refill is brisk.
Test Results: The chest CT scan shows a 2-cm solitary nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe with a smooth appearance and no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that requires further evaluation.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; History of smoking.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT scan to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
The patient will undergo a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will be performed under general anesthesia and will take approximately 1.5 hours.
The patient will be admitted to the hospital for 1 night and will have a chest tube until the following day.
I will continue to monitor the patient's rheumatoid arthritis and recommend continued treatment with methotrexate as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
